_comment: Routing benchmark case study with real before/after Haiku, Opus, and Synthetic small results.

scenes.title: Room Routing Benchmarks
scenes.subtitle: Using live intent fixtures to find cheaper passing routers

scenes.lede: The first live result was bad for a useful reason.
scenes.body: The Oregon Trail general_store.reviewing fixture started at Haiku 2/7, Opus 2/7, and Synthetic small 1/7. The failures exposed a harness/schema contract bug rather than just weak models.

scenes.lede: Kitsoki int slots were being advertised as strings.
scenes.body: Stories author numeric slots as type: int, but the transition schema only mapped JSON Schema integer. Live routers emitted values like oxen:"12" because the tool schema told them that was valid.

scenes.lede: Make the contract precise, then rerun the same room.
scenes.body: The harness now maps int/float/bool shorthand to JSON Schema integer/number/boolean, emits typed examples, and the refine_purchase story distinguishes explicit numeric replacements from vague feedback.

scenes.lede: Cheap room-local routers became viable after the contract fix.
scenes.body: Haiku and Synthetic small passed the validated 7-input fixture. Opus did not: two post-fix attempts both landed at 5/7 with transient 529s and a stable over-conservative bullets slot choice.
Use kitsoki test intents as the gate: deterministic path first, then the cheapest passing room-local model, then fallback. Score the full validated intent call, not just the label.

scenes.lede: Turn the loop into an operating practice.
scenes.body: Add per-fixture reruns, capture Synthetic cost from SDK usage, run 3-5 repetitions per input, and expand the corpus across rooms before promoting models broadly.
